elements.sent-2411-007: We propose one way to transfer the vocabulary and the formalism of thermodynamics (energy, work, heat) and statistical mechanics (partition functions) to statistical inference, starting from Bayes' law.

elements.sent-2411-011: In problems of statistical inference, Bayes'\@ theorem combines the prior information $\pi(\theta)$ on the parameters~$\theta$ of a physical model with the likelihood $\likeli(y|\theta)$ as the distribution of the data points $y$ for a given parameter choice $\theta$ to the posterior distribution $p(\theta|y)$ \begin{equation}\label{eq:BT} p(\theta|y) = \frac{\likeli(y|\theta)\pi(\theta)}{p(y)}\end{equation} with the Bayesian evidence given as the normalisation of $p(\theta|y)$ \begin{equation}p(y) = \smallint\dd^n\theta\:\likeli(y|\theta)\pi(\theta). \label{eq: evidence bayes}\end{equation} We will modify \autoref{eq: evidence bayes , since any consistent definition of integration over parameter space requires a well-defined, invariant volume form $\dd \mu$.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Use of $\dd^n \theta$ is only sufficient in flat, Euclidean space with Cartesian coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-013: The volume form is chosen to be \begin{equation}\dd \mu = \smallint \mathrm{d}^n \theta \:\sqrt{\det F},\end{equation} which correctly reflects the underlying geometry of parameter space for the exponential family at hand.
elements.sent-2411-014: Notably, $\sqrt{\det F}$ is usually referred to as Jeffreys'\@ prior.
elements.sent-2411-015: Based on the presence of the likelihood's Fisher information in $\sqrt{\det F}$, we do not regard this as a prior, but rather as a covolume, which ensures invariance of the volume element under coordinate changes, i.e. under reparameterisations of the statistical model \citep{jeffreysprior101 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Accordingly, we will refer to $\sqrt{\det F}$ as Jeffreys'\@ covolume.
elements.sent-2411-017: Indeed, it is a well-established fact from information geometry that $\sqrt{\det F}$ serves as the invariant covolume of a Riemannian statistical manifold, whose metric is the Fisher information $F$ \citep{jeffreysprior, amari_information_2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: The structure of Bayes'\@ theorem, with an integral in the denominator and the integrand in the numerator, suggests the definition of a partition function (or Bayes partition function) of statistical inference and information theory as \begin{equation}\label{eq:canZ} Z[T,J,n] = \smallint\dd \mu \:\left[\likeli(y|\theta)\pi(\theta)\:\exp(J_\alpha\theta^\alpha)\right]^{1/T}\end{equation} with $T$ a control parameter in analogy to temperature in statistical physics.
elements.sent-2411-019: Here, $T$ is in line with the notion of temperature proposed in mathematical optimisation methods, such as simulated annealing \mbox{\citep{Kirkpatrick .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: A likelihood from the exponential family together with a prior $\pi(\theta) \sim \exp(\varphi(\theta))$ leads to \begin{equation}Z[T,J,n] = \int\dd \mu \:\exp\left(-\frac{1}{T} \left(\Phi(\theta) - J_\alpha\theta^\alpha \right)\right),\label{eq: exponential likeli}\end{equation} allowing to view $\Phi(\theta) := \chi^2(y| \theta)/2 + \varphi(\theta)$ as a potential over parameter space.
elements.sent-2411-021: Indeed, Markov chain Monte Carlo methods, as introduced to cosmology by \citet{lewis_cosmological_2002 , understand the sampler distribution in parameter space as one microscopic realisation of a system subject to~$\Phi$.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Thus, an information theoretical partition function as given in \autoref{eq: exponential likeli governs an ensemble of samplers in the same way that a statistical physics partition would a physical ensemble, completing the correspondence between statistical sampling in inference and their mechanical analogues.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Indeed, the Bayes partition marginalises the microscopic realisations of the system, which are subject to randomness, to access its macroscopic features.
elements.sent-2411-024: Only those features at the macroscopic level, e.g. the moments of the posterior distribution, are relevant for inference.
elements.sent-2411-025: We consider this great structural similarity to be an articulation of a result by \citet{jaynes_information_1957 , who clarifies that thermodynamics is in essence a theory of information: Inverting Jayne's realisation suggests that statistical mechanics and thermodynamics can be used as tools in information-theoretical problems.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Importantly, \autoref{eq: exponential likeli recovers the Bayesian evidence $p(y)$ for $T = 1$ and $J = 0$.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Differentiation of the cumulant generating function $(+ T \ln Z[T,J])$ with respect to $J_\alpha$ gives the cumulants of the posterior distribution $p(\theta|y)$, making them easily accessible at any order \citep{partitionfunction101 .}

elements.section-2411-002: Parametric transition from prior to posterior
elements.sent-2411-041: We begin by viewing a Bayes update in analogy to a continuous transition between two equilibrium ensembles in thermodynamics.
elements.sent-2411-042: To achieve this, a control parameter $\lambda$ is introduced to the Bayes-partition (setting $J=0$), \begin{equation}Z_\lambda[T] = \smallint\dd \mu\: [\likeli(y|\theta)^\lambda \pi(\theta)]^\frac{1}{T}. \label{eq: introduce lambda}\end{equation} For conciseness of notation, we adopt the shorthand $\likeli(y|\theta)^\lambda \pi(\theta) \to \likeli^\lambda \pi$ in this section.
elements.sent-2411-043: Effectively, $\lambda$ is an additional state variable which governs the influence of the data on the inference process.
elements.sent-2411-044: At the same time, \autoref{eq: introduce lambda allows to quantify the rate of change in Shannon's information entropy $S$ with progressing inference ($\lambda: 0 \to 1$) .}
elements.sent-2411-045: Effectively, $\lambda$ describes how the conversion from prior $\pi$ to posterior belief occurs through the influence of the likelihood $\mathcal{L}$ at fixed $T, J$.
elements.sent-2411-046: In thermodynamics, the information entropy $S$ directly follows from the partition function $Z$ via $S(T, (\dots)) = \partial_T (T \ln Z [T,(\dots)])$.
elements.sent-2411-047: Here, $(\dots)$ indicates other control variables, which are not specified further at this point.

elements.sent-2411-292: In problems of statistical inference, Bayes'\@ theorem combines the prior information $\pi(\theta)$ on the parameters~$\theta \in \mathds{R ^n$ of a physical model with the likelihood $\likeli(y|\theta)$ as the distribution of the data points $y$ for a given parameter choice $\theta$ to the posterior distribution $p(\theta|y)$ \begin{equation}\label{eq:BT} p(\theta|y) = \frac{\likeli(y|\theta)\pi(\theta)}{p(y)}\end{equation} with the Bayesian evidence $p(y)$ given as the normalisation of $p(\theta|y)$ \begin{equation}p(y) = \smallint\dd^n\theta\:\likeli(y|\theta)\pi(\theta). \label{eq: evidence bayes}\end{equation} At this instance, one must highlight the fact that use of the integration measure $\smallint \dd^n \theta$ is only justifiable for a flat, Euclidean parameter space with Cartesian coordinates.}
elements.sent-2411-293: However, there is no need for us to make any such strong assumption about the geometry of parameter space.
elements.sent-2411-294: In order to consistently define an invariant volume form $\dd \mu(\theta) $, we turn to the field of information geometry.
